summary:
Fluid Resuscitation

IV Access Basics for the EMT-A Scope

Intravenous (IV) access is a fundamental skill for administering fluids and medications in the prehospital setting. While the specific scope of IV access varies by state and local protocol, many EMT-A (EMT-Basic) levels are now permitted to establish peripheral IV access and administer crystalloid fluids under standing orders or online medical direction. Even where EMS providers do not start IVs, understanding IV basics is essential because you will assist ALS providers and need to monitor IV sites during transport.

  • Cannula selection: Larger-gauge catheters (14–18 gauge) are preferred for trauma and resuscitation because they allow faster flow rates. Smaller gauges (20–22 gauge) may be used for patients with small or fragile veins (elderly, paediatric).
  • Preferred sites: The antecubital fossa (AC) and forearm are the most common sites, followed by the dorsal hand veins. In critical patients, the AC provides the largest access for rapid fluid administration.
  • IV tubing: Standard macrodrip tubing delivers 10–15 drops/mL for rapid administration. Blood tubing (with an in-line filter) is used when transfusing blood products (not typically in the EMT scope).
  • Saline lock (heparin lock): A capped IV catheter that can be flushed with normal saline for intermittent access without continuous IV fluids running.

Crystalloid Fluids: Normal Saline and Lactated Ringer's

Crystalloids are electrolyte solutions that can pass through a semipermeable membrane into the interstitial space. They are the primary resuscitative fluids in prehospital care:

  • Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl): Isotonic crystalloid containing 154 mEq/L of sodium and chloride. It is the most widely used prehospital fluid. Each litre of NS expands plasma volume by approximately 250–300 mL (the remainder moves to the interstitium). Side effect: large volumes can cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (because the chloride concentration is higher than plasma).
  • Lactated Ringer's (LR): Isotonic solution with a composition closer to plasma (sodium, chloride, potassium, calcium, and lactate as a buffer). It is preferred by some protocols for trauma resuscitation because it causes less hyperchloraemic acidosis than NS. It is contraindicated (or used with caution) in patients with liver disease (cannot metabolise lactate) or those with significantly elevated potassium.
  • D5W (5% Dextrose in Water): Not a resuscitation fluid — it is hypotonic and distributes into total body water. Used for maintenance fluids or to correct hypoglycaemia, not for volume expansion in shock.

The 20 mL/kg Bolus

The standard initial fluid bolus for hypotensive patients is 20 mL/kg of isotonic crystalloid. This is a rapid infusion administered as quickly as possible (using pressure bag or manual squeeze if needed). For a 70 kg adult, this equals approximately 1400 mL (one 1000 mL bag + one 500 mL bag or part of a second bag). After the bolus, the patient is reassessed. If signs of shock persist and there are no signs of fluid overload, a second 20 mL/kg bolus may be administered per protocol.

Key Calculation: 20 mL/kg × patient weight (kg) = initial bolus volume. For a 70 kg adult: 20 × 70 = 1400 mL. For an 80 kg adult: 20 × 80 = 1600 mL. For a 50 kg patient: 20 × 50 = 1000 mL. Always estimate weight carefully — a 10 kg error leads to a 200 mL dosing error.

Signs of Fluid Responsiveness

Not all hypotensive patients respond to fluids. Before and after each bolus, assess for clinical signs that indicate whether the patient is "volume responsive":

  • Improvement in mental status: The patient becomes more alert and oriented — a positive sign of volume responsiveness.
  • Improvement in heart rate: Tachycardia decreases toward normal as stroke volume improves.
  • Improvement in blood pressure: SBP rises above 90–100 mmHg and stabilises.
  • Improvement in peripheral perfusion: Capillary refill improves (<2 s), skin becomes warmer and less diaphoretic.
  • Stronger peripheral pulses: Radial pulses that were weak or absent return and strengthen.
  • Increase in urine output: Not assessable in most prehospital settings (requires catheterisation), but useful information if available from a hospitalised patient.

Signs of fluid NON-responsiveness or fluid overload: Continued hypotension after two boluses (suggests ongoing bleeding or non-hypovolaemic shock), worsening respiratory distress, new or worsening crackles on lung auscultation, increasing jugular venous distension (JVD), or the development of peripheral oedema. In a patient with any of these signs, slow or stop fluid administration and consider other causes of shock.

Fluid Warming

Administration of cold IV fluids contributes to hypothermia, which worsens coagulopathy and increases mortality in trauma patients. Fluid warming devices warm crystalloids to approximately 37–40°C before infusion. If a fluid warmer is not available, store IV bags in a warm environment or use an in-line warming system. For non-trauma patients, room-temperature fluids are generally acceptable unless the patient is already hypothermic.

Intraosseous (IO) Access — Overview

Intraosseous access is the alternative vascular access route when IV access cannot be obtained and the patient is critically ill or injured. The IO space (the bone marrow cavity) acts as a non-collapsible vein, allowing rapid absorption of fluids and medications into the central circulation. Key facts:

  • Indications: Cardiac arrest, severe shock with failed IV attempts (typically after 2 attempts or 90 seconds), paediatric emergencies where IV access is difficult, burns with no accessible veins.
  • Insertion sites: Proximal tibia (most common), distal femur, humeral head (gives rapid central-like flow rates), and sternum (used in some military protocols).
  • Device types: Manual needles (Jamshidi), spring-loaded (EZ-IO®), and drill-powered devices.
  • Considerations: Contraindicated in fractured bones, at or above the fracture site, in bones with previous orthopaedic hardware, or in bones with infection (cellulitis/osteomyelitis) over the insertion site.
  • At the EMT-A level: IO access is generally NOT within the basic EMT scope in most states (it is an AEMT/paramedic skill). However, EMTs must understand the indications, sites, and complications to assist the ALS provider.

Caution: Fluid Overload in Cardiogenic Shock

Not all shock is hypovolaemic. Administering large fluid volumes to a patient in cardiogenic shock (pump failure) can cause iatrogenic pulmonary oedema (fluid backing up into the lungs because the heart cannot pump the volume forward). Signs of fluid overload during resuscitation include:

  • Shortness of breath / increased work of breathing
  • Crackles (rales) on lung auscultation — beginning at the bases
  • Pink frothy sputum (late sign — indicates severe pulmonary oedema)
  • Jugular venous distension (JVD)
  • Worsening hypoxia despite increasing oxygen delivery
  • Peripheral oedema (a later sign in acute overload)

If you suspect cardiogenic shock (history of heart disease, chest pain, crackles, JVD, ST-elevation on ECG): give fluids cautiously — a 250–500 mL challenge rather than a full 20 mL/kg bolus — and reassess frequently. The EMS provider's best tool in cardiogenic shock is recognition and rapid transport to a PCI centre.

Reassessment After Each Bolus

Fluid resuscitation is not a 'set and forget' intervention. After every fluid bolus, the EMS provider must reassess:

ParameterWhat to Look ForAction
Mental statusImproving, stable, or worseningDeterioration → re-evaluate oxygenation, perfusion, and need for airway support
Heart rateTrending down vs. unchanged vs. risingNo improvement → consider need for second bolus or blood products
Blood pressure≥90 systolic, MAP ≥65If still low → second bolus or vasopressors (ALS)
Respiratory statusRate, effort, SpO₂, lung soundsNew crackles or JVD → signs of fluid overload → slow/stop fluids
Peripheral pulsesRadial pulse strengthReturn of strong radial pulse = good response
SkinWarmth, colour, moisturePink, warm, dry = improved perfusion

MAST / PASG — Historical Context

The Military Anti-Shock Trousers (MAST) — also called Pneumatic Anti-Shock Garment (PASG) — were large inflatable trousers that wrapped around the patient's legs and abdomen. They were hypothesised to: 1) Autotransfuse blood from the lower extremities to the core (adding approximately 250–500 mL of circulating volume), and 2) Splint pelvic and lower extremity fractures. However, large studies found that MAST/PASG did NOT improve survival and, in some cases, increased mortality (by increasing bleeding in thoracic injuries, causing compartment syndrome, and delaying other care). Modern EMS guidelines have largely abandoned their use. The current standard is haemorrhage control, judicious crystalloid resuscitation, and rapid transport to definitive care.

Historical Lesson: MAST/PASG is an important example of an intervention that made physiological sense in theory — "squeeze blood back to the heart" — but did not survive evidence-based scrutiny. It reminds us to base prehospital practice on outcomes, not assumptions. Today, the emphasis is on early haemorrhage control (tourniquets, haemostatic gauze) and permissive hypotension rather than mechanical autotransfusion.

quiz.q: A 70 kg adult male in haemorrhagic shock requires a 20 mL/kg fluid bolus. Approximately how much volume should be administered?
Which of the following is a contraindication to lactated Ringer's administration?

[HARD] You are treating a 55-year-old female with suspected cardiogenic shock secondary to an anterior MI. Her BP is 86/58, HR 112, and lung sounds reveal crackles halfway up both lung fields. She is in respiratory distress with an SpO₂ of 88% on a non-rebreather. What is the MOST appropriate fluid strategy?
options: Withhold fluids entirely — any fluid administration will cause pulmonary oedema and worsen oxygenation
Administer a 20 mL/kg NS bolus — the hypotension must be treated to perfuse the heart
Give a cautious 250 mL fluid challenge, reassess for worsening respiratory status and signs of overload, and transport emergently to a PCI centre
Administer 500 mL of D5W to provide glucose for myocardial metabolism

flashcards.question: What is the standard initial fluid bolus volume per kilogram for a hypotensive patient?
flashcards.answer: 20 mL/kg of isotonic crystalloid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's)

flashcards.question: What is the primary difference between normal saline and lactated Ringer's?
flashcards.answer: LR has a composition closer to plasma and contains lactate as a buffer; NS has higher chloride content, and large volumes may cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis

flashcards.question: What are three clinical signs that indicate a patient is responding to fluid resuscitation?
flashcards.answer: Improving mental status, decreasing heart rate, and rising blood pressure (also: stronger peripheral pulses, faster cap refill, warmer skin)

flashcards.question: What are two signs of fluid overload that should cause the EMS provider to slow or stop fluid administration?
flashcards.answer: Worsening respiratory distress with new crackles (pulmonary oedema) and increasing jugular venous distension (JVD)

flashcards.question: What is intraosseous (IO) access and when is it indicated?
flashcards.answer: Vascular access through the bone marrow cavity, used when IV access fails in critical patients (cardiac arrest, severe shock, paediatric emergencies)

flashcards.question: What is the primary concern when administering large fluid volumes to a patient with suspected cardiogenic shock?
flashcards.answer: Iatrogenic pulmonary oedema — the failing heart cannot pump the volume forward, causing fluid to back up into the lungs

flashcards.question: Why was the MAST/PASG device largely abandoned in modern EMS?
flashcards.answer: Studies showed it did not improve survival and could increase mortality by worsening bleeding, causing compartment syndrome, and delaying other care

critical.scenario: You are treating a 28-year-old male who was stabbed in the left lower abdomen. The wound is actively bleeding (dark red blood). He is awake but anxious, HR 116, BP 98/72, RR 20, SpO₂ 97%. You apply a pressure dressing to the wound and it slows the bleeding. The patient weight is approximately 80 kg. Per protocol, you may administer a fluid bolus for signs of shock. The receiving trauma centre is 15 minutes away.
critical.question: What fluid resuscitation strategy is MOST appropriate for this patient?

options.t: Establish IV access, administer a 20 mL/kg bolus of NS (≈1600 mL) en route, but titrate to maintain SBP ~80–90 mmHg (permissive hypotension). If BP improves and bleeding does not worsen, no second bolus is needed
options.f: Correct — This patient has compensated (early decompensated) haemorrhagic shock from an abdominal stab wound. The goal is permissive hypotension: enough volume to maintain core perfusion (SBP 80–90) without disrupting formed clots. A full 20 mL/kg bolus is appropriate but infuse judiciously. If BP normalises (>100), slow the infusion — over-resuscitation can increase bleeding by raising hydrostatic pressure on the wound.

options.t: Give the full 20 mL/kg NS bolus as fast as possible — the patient is tachycardic and hypotensive, and needs aggressive volume replacement to restore normal perfusion
options.f: Incorrect — While this patient needs fluids, aggressive resuscitation to normotension can worsen haemorrhage through the 'pop the clot' effect (increased hydrostatic pressure dislodges newly formed clots). Permissive hypotension targets SBP 80–90 mmHg. The wound is abdominal (cannot be tourniqueted), so avoiding clot disruption is critical. Give the bolus but slow the rate once a radial pulse returns and mental status improves.

critical.explanation: In haemorrhagic shock from penetrating trauma, the principle of permissive hypotension applies: fluids are given to maintain adequate (not normal) perfusion. Target: palpable radial pulse, SBP 80–90 mmHg, improving mental status. Administer the 20 mL/kg bolus but titrate the rate based on clinical response. Rapid transport to a trauma centre is the definitive treatment — this patient likely needs surgery for internal haemorrhage control.
critical.kpi: Applies permissive hypotension with appropriate crystalloid dosing in penetrating trauma

critical.scenario: You are treating a 65-year-old male with a history of CHF and COPD who is in respiratory distress. He has crackles throughout all lung fields, JVD, and is coughing up pink frothy sputum. His BP is 110/70, HR 104, RR 28, SpO₂ 84% on a non-rebreather. A paramedic student on scene suggests starting two large-bore IVs and giving a 20 mL/kg NS bolus for the tachycardia and 'relative hypotension.' The patient's weight is approximately 75 kg.
critical.question: What is the correct fluid management for this patient?

options.t: Withhold IV fluids — this patient is in acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (pump failure with fluid overload). Fluids will worsen his condition. The priority is CPAP/BiPAP, nitroglycerin (per protocol), and rapid transport
options.f: Correct — This patient has all the hallmarks of acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema: known CHF, crackles throughout, JVD, pink frothy sputum, hypoxia. This is NOT hypovolaemic shock — the problem is pump failure. IV fluids will worsen pulmonary congestion. The treatment is CPAP (positive pressure ventilation), nitroglycerin (if BP permits and per protocol), and transport. IV access can be established as a 'lifeline' but fluids should not be administered.

options.t: Establish IV access and administer a 250 mL NS challenge — the patient is tachycardic with a normal BP but needs a small fluid challenge to see if there is a hypovolaemic component
options.f: Incorrect — While a 250 mL challenge is theoretically less dangerous than a full bolus, this patient's presentation is unequivocally cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. The crackles throughout all fields and pink frothy sputum indicate severe left heart failure. Any additional fluid will worsen gas exchange. Establish a saline lock for medication access but do not administer a fluid bolus. CPAP and nitroglycerin are the indicated treatments.

critical.explanation: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema is a state of fluid overload, not hypovolaemia. Administering fluids — even small amounts — can precipitate respiratory failure requiring intubation. The EMS provider's priorities: 1) CPAP/BiPAP to improve oxygenation and reduce preload. 2) Nitroglycerin per protocol (vasodilation reduces preload and afterload). 3) Position patient upright. 4) Transport. IV access is for medication administration, not fluid resuscitation.
critical.kpi: Identifies cardiogenic pulmonary oedema and appropriately withholds fluids
